questions.question: Explain the difference between Tree Search and Graph Search versions of BFS. Which one is more efficient and why?
keyPoints: Tree Search does not keep track of visited nodes
Graph Search maintains a CLOSED/Explored set to avoid revisiting
Graph Search is more efficient as it avoids exploring same states multiple times
Graph Search prevents infinite loops in cyclic graphs
Memory trade-off: Graph Search uses more memory but saves time

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of DFS over BFS?
keyPoints: Advantages: Less memory (O(bm) vs O(b^d)), suitable for deep/infinite trees, can find solution quickly if deep
Disadvantages: Not complete for infinite spaces, not optimal (may find long path first), can get stuck in loops
Trade-off: DFS trades optimality for memory efficiency

questions.question: Describe the three main problems faced by Hill Climbing algorithm in search spaces.
keyPoints: Local Maximum: Reaches a peak that is not the global maximum, all neighbors are worse
Plateau: Flat region where all neighboring states have same value, no clear direction to move
Ridge: Steep area where movement is possible only in limited directions, hard to navigate
Solutions: Random restart, stochastic hill climbing, simulated annealing

questions.question: Compare Simple Hill Climbing with Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing.
keyPoints: Simple Hill Climbing: Examines neighbors one by one, moves to first better neighbor found
Steepest-Ascent: Examines all neighbors, selects the best one (highest heuristic value)
Steepest-Ascent is more thorough but slower
Simple Hill Climbing is faster but may miss better solutions
Both can get stuck at local maxima

questions.question: Why is BFS considered complete but not optimal in all cases?
keyPoints: Complete: Always finds a solution if one exists in finite state space
Optimal: Finds shortest path in terms of number of edges
Not optimal for weighted graphs: Does not consider edge costs
For weighted graphs, algorithms like Dijkstra or A* are needed
BFS assumes all edges have equal cost (typically 1)

questions.question: Explain the working of Breadth-First Search algorithm with a suitable example. Also analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Use Queue, start from root, explore all neighbors at current depth before going deeper
Steps: 1) Add start node to queue, 2) While queue not empty, 3) Dequeue node, 4) If goal found return, 5) Add all unvisited neighbors to queue
Time Complexity: O(b^d) where b=branching factor, d=depth of shallowest goal
Space Complexity: O(b^d) - must store frontier and explored nodes
Properties: Complete (finite space), Optimal (unweighted graphs)
Example with diagram: Show level-by-level expansion

questions.question: Explain Hill Climbing algorithm in detail. Discuss its variants and how they address the limitations of basic Hill Climbing.
keyPoints: Hill Climbing is greedy local search using heuristic evaluation function
Algorithm: Generate successor, if better move there, else stop
Types: Simple (first better), Steepest-ascent (best better), Stochastic (random better), Random restart
Limitations: Local maxima, plateaus, ridges
How variants address: Random restart escapes local maxima by trying new starts; Stochastic adds randomness; Steepest picks best neighbor
Does not maintain search tree or history - only current state
Not complete or optimal in general

questions.question: Compare BFS, DFS, and Hill Climbing search algorithms under the following criteria: (a) Completeness (b) Optimality (c) Time Complexity (d) Space Complexity (e) Use Cases
keyPoints: (a) Completeness: BFS-complete for finite; DFS-not complete for infinite; Hill Climbing-not complete
(b) Optimality: BFS-optimal for unweighted; DFS-not optimal; Hill Climbing-not optimal
(c) Time: BFS-O(b^d), DFS-O(bm), Hill Climbing-O(b*m)
(d) Space: BFS-O(b^d), DFS-O(bm), Hill Climbing-O(b)
(e) Use Cases: BFS-shortest path unweighted; DFS-memory limited, deep search; Hill Climbing-optimization problems, local search

questions.question: Write the pseudocode for BFS and DFS algorithms. Explain how they differ in terms of data structures used and exploration order.
keyPoints: BFS Pseudocode: Queue-based, level-order traversal
DFS Pseudocode: Stack-based or recursive, depth-order traversal
Data Structures: BFS uses FIFO Queue; DFS uses LIFO Stack or recursion
Exploration Order: BFS expands shallowest nodes first; DFS expands deepest nodes first
Key difference: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted; DFS may find longer path first
Both maintain OPEN (frontier) and CLOSED (explored) lists
